perturbation.original_text: 1.2. “Confidential Information” means information related to a Party, its Group Companies, its Authorized Persons, this Agreement or any agreement to which this Agreement is annexed or upon which this Agreement’s Purpose is based , that a Party receives or accesses , except information that (i) is or falls into the public domain without fault of the Receiving Party , (ii) the Receiving Party can prove was in its possession without any obligation of confidentiality prior to receipt from the Disclosing Party , (iii) is independently developed by the Receiving Party , or (iv) is obtained by the Receiving Party from third parties without any obligation of confidentiality to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2. “Confidential Information” means information related to a Party, its Group Companies, its Authorized Persons, this Agreement or any agreement to which this Agreement’s Purpose is based , that a Party receives or accesses , except information that (i) is or falls into the public domain without fault of the Receiving Party , (ii) the Receiving Party can prove was in its possession without any obligation of confidentiality prior to receipt from the Disclosing Party , (iii) is independently developed by the Receiving Party .
perturbation.explanation: By removing exception (iv), which states that information obtained from third parties without an obligation of confidentiality is *not* Confidential Information, it now contradicts Section 3.4 (Non-disclosure), because any third-party information the Receiving Party obtains is automatically considered Confidential Information, regardless of existing confidentiality obligations or lack thereof. It is now unclear if the Receiving party has to consider information that was already in public domain as confidential, due to the change.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.4. Non-disclosure . Each Party shall hold the other Party ’s Confidential Information in strictest confidence and shall not disclose the other Party ’s Confidential Information to any third party , except to the Receiving Party’s own Authorized Persons on a need -to-know basis .
perturbation.justification: The original text defined 'Confidential Information' with exceptions, including (iv) information obtained from third parties without confidentiality obligations. The changed text omits exception (iv). This omission directly contradicts Section 3.4, 'Non-disclosure,' which requires all Confidential Information to be held in strictest confidence. Now, any third-party information received is considered Confidential Information, regardless of pre-existing confidentiality obligations, creating a conflict. This creates a clear inconsistency regarding which information falls under the non-disclosure obligations, leading to a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 1.5. “Expiration Date ” means 1 year from the Effective Date.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.5. “Expiration Date ” means the date defined in writing by Teqball Holding.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the definition of 'Expiration Date' to a date defined by Teqball Holding without a maximum period, it creates a contradiction with Section 3.2 (Exchange of Confidential Information), which speaks of exchanging information up to the 'Expiration Date.' This makes it potentially indefinite and gives Teqball Holding unilateral power to extend the confidentiality obligations indefinitely, which is unfair and unclear.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.2. Exchange of Confidential Information . Confidential Information may be exchanged by the Parties from the Effective Date until the Expiration Date, or, if the agreement(s) underpinning the Purpose have been entered into before the Expiration Date, then Confidential Info rmation may be exchanged until the obligations in such agreement(s) are still in effect.
perturbation.justification: The original text defined 'Expiration Date' as a fixed period (1 year from the Effective Date). The changed text replaces this with a definition determined by Teqball Holding, introducing uncertainty and the potential for an indefinite period. This directly contradicts Section 3.2, which uses 'Expiration Date' to limit the exchange of confidential information. The change gives Teqball Holding unilateral power to extend confidentiality obligations indefinitely, creating a strong contradiction and potential unfairness.

perturbation.original_text: 3.6. Breach of Confidentiality . The Receiving Party shall immediately notify the Disclosing Party upon learning of any actual or potential access of its Confidential Information by unauthorized third parties. If (i) an Authorized Person’s or former Authorized Person’s action or omission would be considered a breach of this Agreement if they had been a party thereto, and (ii) the Receiving Party has coercive power over them at the time of such action or omission, then the Receiving Party shall be responsible for such action or omission as if it were its own breach .
perturbation.changed_text: 3.6. Breach of Confidentiality . The Receiving Party shall immediately notify the Disclosing Party upon learning of any actual or potential access of its Confidential Information by unauthorized third parties. If (i) an Authorized Person’s or former Authorized Person’s action or omission would be considered a breach of this Agreement if they had been a party thereto, and (ii) the Receiving Party has coercive power over them at the time of such action or omission, then the Receiving Party may be responsible for such action or omission as if it were its own breach .
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be responsible' to 'may be responsible' introduces ambiguity regarding the Receiving Party's liability for breaches caused by Authorized Persons. It contradicts the intention of holding the Receiving Party accountable for the actions of those they authorize to access Confidential Information. Now, the liability is discretionary.
perturbation.location: 3. Non-disclos ure and Non-use O bligations
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.6. Breach of Confidentiality . The Receiving Party shall immediately notify the Disclosing Party upon learning of any actual or potential access of its Confidential Information by unauthorized third parties. If (i) an Authorized Person’s or former Authorized Person’s action or omission would be considered a breach of this Agreement if they had been a party thereto, and (ii) the Receiving Party has coercive power over them at the time of such action or omission, then the Receiving Party may be responsible for such action or omission as if it were its own breach .
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that the Receiving Party 'shall be responsible' for breaches caused by Authorized Persons. The changed text modifies this to 'may be responsible.' This introduces a clear contradiction because the original text created a mandatory obligation, while the changed text makes the Receiving Party's responsibility discretionary. This directly conflicts with the intent of holding the Receiving Party accountable, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation, leading to ambiguity and potential unenforceability. The Contradicted text is the same as the Changed text, solidifying this contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2.2. Governing Law and Venue . All aspects of this Agreement, all matters arising from it, including disputes or lawsuits, and the relationship of the Parties relating to this Agreement, are governed by the laws of Luxembourg , and its courts shall have exclusive jurisdiction thereover . Nevertheless, each Party recognizes that a breach of confidentiality may cause irreparable harm to the other and that actual damages may be difficult to ascertain or inadequate in such cases. Each Party shall thus have the right to enforce this Agreemen t by injunction, specific performance, or other equitable relief, without a requirement to prove actual damages and without prejudice to any other rights and remedies that they may have otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.2. Governing Law and Venue . All aspects of this Agreement, all matters arising from it, including disputes or lawsuits, and the relationship of the Parties relating to this Agreement, are governed by the laws of Luxembourg , and its courts shall have exclusive jurisdiction thereover . Nevertheless, each Party recognizes that a breach of confidentiality may cause irreparable harm to the other and that actual damages may be difficult to ascertain or inadequate in such cases. Each Party shall thus have the right to enforce this Agreemen t by injunction, specific performance, or other equitable relief, with a requirement to prove actual damages and without prejudice to any other rights and remedies that they may have otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'without a requirement to prove actual damages' to 'with a requirement to prove actual damages,' this completely contradicts the clause's original intention and the inherent nature of an NDA, which is designed to protect against potential and hard-to-quantify harm. This modification undermines the effectiveness of injunctive relief by placing the burden on the Disclosing Party to demonstrate concrete financial damages, which is often extremely difficult in cases of confidential information leaks. This modification of course would mean that an NDA would not be the optimal choice for this specific agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.2. Governing Law and Venue . All aspects of this Agreement, all matters arising from it, including disputes or lawsuits, and the relationship of the Parties relating to this Agreement, are governed by the laws of Luxembourg , and its courts shall have exclusive jurisdiction thereover . Nevertheless, each Party recognizes that a breach of confidentiality may cause irreparable harm to the other and that actual damages may be difficult to ascertain or inadequate in such cases. Each Party shall thus have the right to enforce this Agreemen t by injunction, specific performance, or other equitable relief, with a requirement to prove actual damages and without prejudice to any other rights and remedies that they may have otherwise.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that a party can seek injunctive relief 'without a requirement to prove actual damages'. The changed text alters this to 'with a requirement to prove actual damages'. This directly contradicts the purpose of the original clause and undermines the enforceability of the agreement, especially regarding breaches of confidentiality where damages are difficult to quantify. The contradicted text is exactly the same as the changed text in this case, but the explanation given outlines how the Change causes a contradiction in the document itself.
